exercises.title: HTTP Status Code Classifier
exercises.description: Write a function `classify_status(code)` that takes an HTTP status code (integer) and returns its category as a string: '2xx Success', '3xx Redirect', '4xx Client Error', '5xx Server Error', or 'Unknown'. Then print the classification for codes: 200, 301, 404, 500, 418.
exercises.starterCode:
# TODO: Write a function that classifies HTTP status codes
def classify_status(code):
    # TODO: Use integer division (code // 100) to get the category
    # Return '2xx Success', '3xx Redirect', '4xx Client Error',
    # '5xx Server Error', or 'Unknown'
    pass

# TODO: Print the classification for each of these codes
codes = [200, 301, 404, 500, 418]
for code in codes:
    print(f"{code}: {classify_status(code)}")
exercises.expectedOutput:
200: 2xx Success
301: 3xx Redirect
404: 4xx Client Error
500: 5xx Server Error
418: 4xx Client Error
hints: Use integer division: code // 100 gives you 2 for 200-299, 3 for 300-399, etc.
A dictionary mapping integer keys to category strings works cleanly here.
418 is a real status code (I'm a teapot) — it falls in the 4xx range.

exercises.title: URL Parser
exercises.description: Parse a URL string into its components using only string methods (no `urllib`). Given the URL `'https://api.example.com:443/users/123?format=json'`, extract and print: scheme, host, port, path, and query string.
exercises.starterCode:
url = "https://api.example.com:443/users/123?format=json"

# TODO: Extract each component using string methods
# Hint: split on '://', then '/', then ':', then '?'

# TODO: Print each component on its own line in this format:
# scheme: https
# host: api.example.com
# port: 443
# path: /users/123
# query: format=json
exercises.expectedOutput:
scheme: https
host: api.example.com
port: 443
path: /users/123
query: format=json
hints: Split on '://' first to separate the scheme from the rest.
After removing the scheme, split on '/' to isolate the host:port part.
Split on '?' to separate the path from the query string.
Split on ':' to separate the host from the port.

exercises.title: HTTP Request/Response Simulator
exercises.description: Simulate an HTTP request-response cycle using dictionaries. Create a function `handle_request(request)` that takes a request dict with keys `method` and `path`, and returns a response dict with keys `status`, `status_text`, and `body`. Handle: GET '/' → 200, GET '/about' → 200, GET '/missing' → 404, POST '/data' → 201, DELETE '/data' → 405 (Method Not Allowed). Print each response's status and body.
exercises.starterCode:
# Simulated route table
ROUTES = {
    "/": "Welcome to the homepage!",
    "/about": "About this website.",
}

def handle_request(request):
    method = request["method"]
    path = request["path"]
    # TODO: Return a response dict with 'status', 'status_text', 'body'
    # GET on existing path → 200 OK
    # GET on missing path → 404 Not Found
    # POST on /data → 201 Created
    # DELETE on any path → 405 Method Not Allowed
    pass

# TODO: Send these requests and print "STATUS status_text: body" for each
requests = [
    {"method": "GET", "path": "/"},
    {"method": "GET", "path": "/about"},
    {"method": "GET", "path": "/missing"},
    {"method": "POST", "path": "/data"},
    {"method": "DELETE", "path": "/data"},
]
exercises.expectedOutput:
200 OK: Welcome to the homepage!
200 OK: About this website.
404 Not Found: Page not found
201 Created: Resource created
405 Method Not Allowed: Method not supported
hints: Check the method first — DELETE always returns 405 regardless of path.
POST to /data always returns 201 regardless of whether it exists.
For GET, check if the path is in ROUTES; return 200 with the value or 404.
Build the response as a dict: {'status': 200, 'status_text': 'OK', 'body': '...'}.

warmup.title: Quick Recall: String Methods
warmup.description: What does this print? Trace through the code mentally before running it.
warmup.starterCode:
url = "GET /api/users HTTP/1.1"
parts = url.split(" ")
print(parts[0])
print(parts[1].split("/")[2])
print(parts[2].split("/")[0])
